content.text:
{In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.}{John 1:1}
 {The study of mathematics, like the Nile, begins in minuteness but ends in magnificence.}{— Charles Caleb Colton}

Consider the following problem: A coffee shop sells lattes for \$4 each and 
muffins for \$3 each. Yesterday, they made \$170 from selling a total of 50 
items. How many lattes and how many muffins did they sell?
If $x$ represents lattes and $y$ represents muffins, we need:

This is a **system of linear equations**—two or more linear equations 
that must be satisfied simultaneously. Finding the solution means finding 
values of $x$ and $y$ that make *both* equations true at the same time.

content.title: Linear Equation
content.content:
A **linear equation** in the variables 
$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is an equation that can be written in the form
\[
a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 + \cdots + a_n x_n = b
\]
where $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ and $b$ are real numbers (or complex numbers). 
The numbers $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ are called the **coefficients**, 
and $b$ is called the **constant term**.

content.text:
The key feature of a linear equation is that each variable appears to the 
first power only, and there are no products of variables.

content.title: Identifying linear equations
content.problem:
Which of the following are linear equations?

1. $3x - 5y + 2z = 7$

2. $x^2 + y = 1$

3. $xy = 6$

4. $\sqrt{2}\,x_1 - \pi x_2 + e\,x_3 = 0$

5. $\sin(x) + \cos(y) = 1$
content.solution:
1. **Linear.** Each variable appears to the first power.

2. **Not linear.** The term $x^2$ is not first-degree.

3. **Not linear.** The term $xy$ is a product of variables.

4. **Linear.** The coefficients $\sqrt{2}$, $-\pi$, and $e$ are 
 just constants, even though they are irrational.

5. **Not linear.** The functions $\sin$ and $\cos$ are not linear 
 operations on the variables.

content.title: System of Linear Equations
content.content:
A **system of linear equations** 
(or **linear system**) is a collection of one or more linear equations 
in the same variables.

A **solution** of a linear system is an assignment of 
values to the variables that makes every equation in the system true 
simultaneously.

The **solution set** is the set of all solutions. 
Two systems are called **equivalent** if 
they have the same solution set.

content.title: Finding a solution
content.problem:
Verify that $(x, y, z) = (2, -1, 3)$ is a solution to the system
$$
x + 2y + z &= 3 \\
2x - y + 3z &= 14 \\
x + y - z &= -2
$$
content.solution:
We substitute $x = 2$, $y = -1$, $z = 3$ into each equation:
$$
(2) + 2(-1) + (3) &= 2 - 2 + 3 = 3 \quad\checkmark \\
2(2) - (-1) + 3(3) &= 4 + 1 + 9 = 14 \quad\checkmark \\
(2) + (-1) - (3) &= 2 - 1 - 3 = -2 \quad\checkmark
$$
All three equations are satisfied, confirming this is a solution.

content.text:
In two variables, each linear equation represents a **line** in the 
$xy$-plane. The solution to a system of two equations is the 
**intersection** of two lines.

There are exactly three possibilities for a system of two linear equations 
in two unknowns:

items:
**One solution:** The lines intersect at exactly one point. 
 The system is **consistent** and 
 **independent**.

**No solution:** The lines are parallel and never meet. 
 The system is **inconsistent**.

**Infinitely many solutions:** The lines are identical 
 (every point on one is also on the other). The system is 
 **consistent** and **dependent**.

content.text:
In three variables, each linear equation represents a **plane** in 
three-dimensional space. The solution to a system is where the planes 
intersect. Three planes might meet at a single point, along a line, 
everywhere (if all three are identical), or not at all.

content.title: Existence and Uniqueness
content.content:
A system of linear equations has either:

1. No solution (inconsistent), or

2. Exactly one solution, or

3. Infinitely many solutions.
A system is **consistent** if it has at least one solution.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Notice that there is no option for "exactly two solutions" or "exactly 
seventeen solutions." For linear systems, the number of solutions is 
always zero, one, or infinite. This fundamental fact will be explained 
when we study the structure of solution sets in Section [sec:solution-sets].

marginNotes.text:
When he established the heavens, I was there; 
when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.
